public_functions.behavior_description: Initialize the GTM for a 3-phase inverter PWM using the unified IfxGtm_Pwm driver in center-aligned complementary mode at 20 kHz with 1 µs rising/falling dead time, with synchronous start and synchronous update enabled. Algorithm steps: 1) Declare all configuration objects as locals: main IfxGtm_Pwm_Config, an InterruptConfig object for the base channel, per-channel configuration objects (3 logical channels for the 3 complementary pairs), and an outputs array describing high-side pins and their complementary low-side pins. Also reference a persistent module-state struct (in this module's .c file) that holds: the IfxGtm_Pwm handle, a persistent channels[] array that the driver will reference beyond init, and arrays for dutyCycles[3], phases[3], and deadTimes[3]. 2) Call IfxGtm_Pwm_initConfig to load safe defaults into the main config. 3) Configure the outputs in the main config to use complementary pairing for 3 channels, with low-side inverted polarity and hardware dead-time enabled via the GTM DTM associated with the TOM submodule. Set dead-time to 1 µs for both rising and falling edges using the driver's dead-time configuration fields. Configure PWM mode as center-aligned and enable synchronous start (syncStart=true) and synchronous update (the driver’s sync update control). Select the TOM submodule/cluster that provides three logical channels; use the first FXCLK as the clock source in the config. 4) Fill the per-channel configuration entries (channelConfig[0..2]) using logical channel indices SubModule_Ch_0, SubModule_Ch_1, SubModule_Ch_2 and assign the interrupt configuration only to channelConfig[0] (base channel), with the remaining channelConfig entries’ interrupt pointers as NULL. Populate the InterruptConfig with mode=appropriate pulse notify, isrProvider=CPU0, priority=20, and assign the periodEvent callback pointer to IfxGtm_periodEventFunction (void *data). 5) Inside an enable guard, check IfxGtm_isEnabled; if not enabled, call IfxGtm_enable to enable the GTM, read the module clock dynamically with IfxGtm_Cmu_getModuleFrequency (used for subsequent timing derivations) and enable GTM clocks with IfxGtm_Cmu_enableClocks to ensure the needed functional clocks (e.g., FXCLK for TOM/DTM) are active. 6) Call IfxGtm_Pwm_init with the persistent handle, the persistent channels[] array from the module state (not a local array), and the configured main config. The driver applies the per-channel configuration, sets up complementary routing and DTM dead-time, and prepares synchronized start/update. 7) Store initial duty cycle percentages in the module state array exactly as percentages: {25.0f, 50.0f, 75.0f}. Do not apply them in init; they are for the first runtime update call. Store the configured dead-times and phases in the state as needed. 8) Configure the LED GPIO as push-pull output via IfxPort_setPinModeOutput so it can be toggled in the ISR. No STM timing or scheduling logic is included here. Mapping TODO: Confirm exact TOM1 channel-to-TOUT pin mapping for phases U, V, and W (logical channels SubModule_Ch_0, SubModule_Ch_1, SubModule_Ch_2) before finalizing pin assignments; document the confirmed mapping in code comments.

Implement percentage-based duty update for the 3 logical PWM channels (one per complementary pair) using the rule: for each channel i in {0,1,2}, newDuty = oldDuty + step; if newDuty >= 100 then newDuty = 0; store newDuty back to state. Use a constant step of 10 percent. After all three elements have been updated, pass the state’s dutyCycles[3] array directly to the driver’s immediate update API to apply the new percentages to the PWM hardware with synchronous shadow-transfer semantics managed by the driver.

ISR body for the GTM PWM-routed interrupt. Keep the body minimal: toggle the LED output pin and return. No driver-level interrupt handler is called from the ISR body. The ISR is declared with IFX_INTERRUPT elsewhere with provider CPU0 and priority 20, and is routed by the high-level PWM driver’s interrupt configuration.

Empty period-event callback required by the high-level PWM driver’s interrupt configuration. It must take a void *data parameter. Do not perform LED toggling here; the LED action is done exclusively in the ISR. Suppress unused parameter warnings by casting data to void if needed.
